lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators that repeatedly clean, press, finish, receive, collect, or deliver customer-owned garments and household textiles through staffed plants, stores, routes, or drop sites.
lens.excluded_activities: Coin- or card-operated self-service laundries, linen and uniform rental, carpet and upholstery cleaning, captive institutional laundries, passive drop-site property entities, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Households and small commercial accounts usually pay per item, per bag, by weight, or through recurring pickup and delivery plans for garment and textile cleaning.

a.bridge: O*NET's laundry and dry-cleaning task inventory is translated into a wage-weighted exposure share: intake coding, order records, customer communication, scheduling, exception triage, stain and fabric recognition, process-setting suggestions, and quality inspection are treated as partly AI-exposed; loading, unloading, sorting, folding, pressing, spot treatment, maintenance, and material movement are mostly physical. O*NET provides tasks rather than time shares and omits front-office occupations, so the resulting production-heavy mapping likely biases total exposure downward.
a.rationale: Computer vision and workflow software can assist classification, inspection, customer service, routing, and machine settings, but most plant labor manipulates irregular textiles or performs physical finishing and stain work that software alone cannot substitute.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across retail dry-cleaning plants, wet-cleaning laundries, pickup routes, and drop stores separating already automated work from intake, spotting, sorting, finishing, quality control, delivery, and administration.
caveats: O*NET covers laundry workers across household, industrial, and institutional settings rather than this NAICS lens alone.
The task source provides no wage or time weights and underrepresents sales, route, and office work.
EPA's description applies only to facilities using PCE and not to wet-cleaning or other solvent systems.

rho.rationale: Scheduling, messaging, order entry, recommendations, and visual quality checks can be added to modern point-of-sale and plant systems, but reliable handling of varied garments needs machinery, cameras, integration, labeled defect data, human escalation, and capital that many small operators lack. The mandated PCE transition may compete for investment funds.
rho.change_evidence: Production telemetry from dry-cleaning point-of-sale, conveyor, vision-inspection, robotics, and route vendors showing adoption cost, error rates, labor hours removed, override rates, and payback by plant size.
caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation in independent dry cleaners.
Plant age and information-system quality vary materially.
Regulatory equipment replacement can either accelerate modernization or crowd out automation investment.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition largely matches repeat external cleaning services. Ineligible records are mainly owner-dependent shops without transferable systems, passive drop sites, captive or misclassified operations, and entities outside the target band; plant and storefront entities may also duplicate one operating firm.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census linking 812320 establishments to common ownership, plant capacity, routes, normalized EBITDA, customer mix, management depth, environmental liabilities, and transferable leases and permits.
caveats: The code combines cleaning plants, drop stores, pickup sites, specialty cleaners, and laundries with different transferability.
Environmental liabilities and below-market real-estate leases can make an otherwise operating firm ineligible.
The supplied firm estimate is margin-bridged and may count establishments or affiliated entities differently from acquisition targets.

s5.rationale: Local route density, plant utilization, and customer files can create buyer value, but no source provides a dry-cleaning-specific transaction denominator. Small owner-operated stores may close or transfer assets informally rather than undergo a qualifying control sale.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from specialist dry-cleaning brokers, BizBuySell, SBA loan records, and state plant registries, deduplicated to operating firms and matched to closures, owner age, permits, and eligible EBITDA.
caveats: No observed deal-flow or owner-succession rate is available for the lens.
Store sales may exclude the processing plant or customer routes and may not transfer a durable operation.
PCE conversion and environmental diligence can turn intended sales into closures.

q.rationale: Per-item and subscription prices are not contractually tied to labor hours, so efficient operators can initially retain savings. Local price comparison, low switching costs, route promotions, service recovery, and customer expectations for faster turnaround should pass part of the benefit through.
q.change_evidence: Matched operator cohorts showing automation-driven payroll changes, realized item prices, discounts, turnaround, remake claims, route density, churn, and contribution margin by service type.
caveats: No public evidence measures automation benefit retention in retail garment care.
Premium specialty cleaning and commodity wash-and-fold have different pricing power.
Savings may be absorbed by solvent transition, utilities, rent, insurance, and delivery costs.

d5.bridge: BLS projects laundry and dry-cleaning worker employment to grow 5.4% from 2024 to 2034. Taking the square root of 1.054 yields an approximately 1.03 five-year occupational-employment factor, which is adjusted downward to 0.95 for service quantity because the occupation includes growing institutional, hospitality, and linen operations outside consumer garment cleaning and because labor productivity is not constant-quality demand. The broad occupational population likely biases demand for 812320 upward.
d5.rationale: Pickup laundry and household outsourcing may grow, but casual dress, washable garments, home equipment, and environmental transition pressure traditional dry cleaning. The wide range reflects the code's mix of declining garment care and potentially growing wash-and-fold services.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year inflation-adjusted pounds, items, orders, and active-customer counts from dry-cleaning point-of-sale vendors and industry associations, split between dry cleaning, wet cleaning, wash-and-fold, specialty items, stores, and delivery routes.
caveats: Occupational employment is not constant-price, constant-quality service demand.
The occupation includes industries and customer segments outside 812320.
The split between traditional dry cleaning and pickup laundry is not observed.

o.rationale: Customers can home-launder, use self-service machines, or shift to washable garments, but textiles that remain in the service still require accountable custody, cleaning chemistry, machinery, stain treatment, finishing, quality control, and return logistics. AI can reduce labor but cannot provide the physical cleaning service by itself.
o.change_evidence: Customer and operator cohorts measuring substitution to home care or self-service, automated-plant staffing, damaged or lost item claims, human exception rates, and the share of orders completed without skilled spotting or finishing.
caveats: Operator requirement is lower for standardized wash-and-fold than for specialty garments.
Robotic sorting and finishing could advance faster than assumed.
PCE restrictions change cleaning technology but do not directly indicate future customer quantity.

narrative.executive_view: Garment care has a high labor share, but most plant labor is physical handling, finishing, and exception work rather than purely cognitive administration. AI can improve intake, visual inspection, routing, customer service, and process control while regulatory equipment transition and uneven demand limit implementation.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can classify garments and stains, suggest treatment, detect finishing defects, forecast workload, automate order messages, optimize routes, and assist pricing and scheduling. Loading, sorting, pressing, spotting, folding, maintenance, and accountable custody remain equipment- and labor-intensive.
narrative.value_capture: Per-item and subscription pricing permits some savings retention, but low switching costs and local competition encourage faster service, promotions, pickup convenience, and lower effective prices. Capital and compliance costs may consume much of the gain.
narrative.firm_availability: The code broadly fits the repeat-service lens, yet plant, route, storefront, and specialty-cleaning models differ materially. Environmental liabilities, owner dependence, and affiliated drop sites reduce the share of countable firms that are transferable platforms.
narrative.demand_durability: Household laundry outsourcing and pickup delivery can support volume, while traditional dry cleaning faces washable clothing, casual dress, and home laundering. The physical service survives for selected garments and convenience users, but mix shift is central.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are physical-task dominance, unproven robotics economics, PCE replacement costs, contaminated-site liabilities, water and energy expense, garment damage, route density, lease transfer, owner dependence, and the absence of segment-level volume or transaction data.
narrative.principal_driver: A meaningful labor bill and repetitive intake, inspection, scheduling, and customer workflows create a bounded automation opportunity around the physical plant.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most production work handles irregular textiles physically, while traditional dry-cleaning demand and regulatory equipment costs can erode the benefit.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 812320 Drycleaning and Laundry Services (except Coin-Operated)
sources.supports: Defines staffed dry-cleaning, laundry, pickup and drop-off, and specialty garment-cleaning activities and distinguishes self-service laundry, linen supply, and carpet cleaning.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Laundry and Dry-Cleaning Workers
sources.supports: Lists machine loading and operation, sorting and counting, marking, stain and fabric inspection, spotting, pressing, folding, packaging, maintenance, and material-handling tasks.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Projections and Worker Characteristics, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects laundry and dry-cleaning worker employment from 202.6 thousand in 2024 to 213.5 thousand in 2034, a 5.4% increase, across the broad occupation.

sources.title: EPA Guide to Complying with the 2024 PCE Dry Cleaning Regulation
sources.supports: Describes manual loading, unloading, stain removal, solvent handling, maintenance, recordkeeping, alternative cleaning systems, and the staged prohibition of PCE in dry cleaning.
